candidate_problems.text: Can the conjecture be made non-circular by deriving, rather than stipulating, that low-entropy boundary conditions are genuinely necessary for any open-ended knowledge growth, records, and irreversible complexity? The key unresolved tension is whether 'usable disequilibrium' is a deep necessity across all possible physics or only a contingent feature of worlds like ours; without this, the claim risks redescribing our universe's requirements instead of explaining them.

What concrete lawlike mechanism could make a low-entropy beginning generic rather than an unexplained special case? The open problem is to formulate and compare candidate frameworks—boundary laws, measures over histories, cosmological reproduction/selection, etc.—that actually yield robust low-entropy, record-bearing worlds without smuggling in fine-tuned assumptions at a deeper level.

Is the public-goods analogy explanatorily substantive or merely metaphorical when applied to cosmology? The unresolved question is how to rigorously map local incentives, non-excludability, and no-provision equilibria onto physical law, given that the universe is not a population of strategic agents; resolving this matters because the conjecture's force depends on whether the analogy identifies real structural constraints or just offers an evocative redescription.
